author: @michaelcasper4727
text: My experience with bike generation (in the first contest #11) almost to a tee.  I had magnets around the rim and coils at the top but not enough power coming out.  I had to change my plan a bit and go with a motor.  Of course, the generator has more magnet weight than the 36 magnets I used (and only one side) and more winding weight than the five coils planned for use.  Luke is smart in using voltage to control how hard to pedal but, I have a DC motor, so I used the bike gearing to control torque pedaling.  Also, like how Luke used thin board to mount the alternator - much like mine.

author: @cosmiccrunch8591
text: Why does the alternator need power ? I thought it was just the rotor that needed power to operate the induction brake and increase physical resistance?

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text:
The typical automotive alternator is a variable output device and the rotor isn't magnetic until the voltage regulator senses/determines the required load. At that point the voltage regulator provides an appropriate voltage to the rotor to magnetize so it can produce the required amount of  power.
In a nutshell, the output from the alternator is determined by the voltage applied to the rotor....up to it's rated limits of course.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text: @marthale7 not exactly, it applies power to the rotor making it an electromagnet....power isn't applied to "the coils"

author: @electronresonator8882
text: electromagnets, from hydro power plant to petrol generator set, they use variable inductance to create more or less power...it's better way to produce power than simply relying on static permanent magnetic strength....but for motor, magnet is the better way to create torque
